coredata.dc:title: Rational design of Boerhavia diffusa derived CoFe2O4-Carbon dots@Boehmite platform for photocatalysis and ultra trace monitoring of hazardous pesticide and UO2 2+ ions

coredata.dc:description: In view of exploiting natural resources for designing of effectual materials in favor of detection and obliteration of water pollutants, a fluorescent nanomaterial (CDBHCF) based on biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) was constructed. The CDs and cobalt ferrite (CF) particles were anchored on boehmite (BH) which served as a support material for CDs. The CDBHCF nanocomposite was prepared via facile hydrothermal treatment for selective recognition of Methyl parathion (MP) pesticide and uranyl ions (UO2 2+). The corresponding structural, morphological and opto-electronic properties of the nanomaterials have been investigated by different physicochemical techniques. The fluorescent CDBHCF probe was employed to detect extremely low concentration of MP and UO2 2+ with detection limit of 22.4 nM and 4.4 nM, respectively. Ultimately, the proposed sensing platform was validated through real sample analysis. Besides, CDBHCF nanocomposite was utilized for photocatalytic abolition of Tetracycline (TC) in water samples. Initially, the impact of various operational parameters on the degradation efficiency, including catalyst dosage and initial pH were thoroughly examined. Under optimized conditions, the fabricated CDBHCF nanocomposite demonstrated excellent results for photocatalytic degradation of TC (92 % degradation in 120 min) under visible light illumination. Thus, the proposed strategy delivered an innovative insight for dual purpose of CDBHCF nanocomposite: as a fluorescent probe for real time monitoring and as a photocatalyst for removal of pollutants via simple photocatalytic degradation.

head.ce:title: Rational design of Boerhavia diffusa derived CoFe2O4-Carbon dots@Boehmite platform for photocatalysis and ultra trace monitoring of hazardous pesticide and UO2 2+ ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional platform was contrived through assimilation of highly fluorescent Boerhavia diffusa weed derived carbon dots, CoFe2O4 and boehmite nanoparticles (CoFe2O4-CD@Boehmite) for the trace level fluorescence detection of lethal pesticide Methyl parathion (MP) and uranyl ions (UO2 2+) ions in aqueous sample. Besides the formed nanocomposite was methodically studied for photocatalytic degradation of toxic Tetracycline (TC) antibiotic.

In view of exploiting natural resources for designing of effectual materials in favor of detection and obliteration of water pollutants, a fluorescent nanomaterial (CDBHCF) based on biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) was constructed. The CDs and cobalt ferrite (CF) particles were anchored on boehmite (BH) which served as a support material for CDs. The CDBHCF nanocomposite was prepared via facile hydrothermal treatment for selective recognition of Methyl parathion (MP) pesticide and uranyl ions (UO2 2+). The corresponding structural, morphological and opto-electronic properties of the nanomaterials have been investigated by different physicochemical techniques. The fluorescent CDBHCF probe was employed to detect extremely low concentration of MP and UO2 2+ with detection limit of 22.4 nM and 4.4 nM, respectively. Ultimately, the proposed sensing platform was validated through real sample analysis. Besides, CDBHCF nanocomposite was utilized for photocatalytic abolition of Tetracycline (TC) in water samples. Initially, the impact of various operational parameters on the degradation efficiency, including catalyst dosage and initial pH were thoroughly examined. Under optimized conditions, the fabricated CDBHCF nanocomposite demonstrated excellent results for photocatalytic degradation of TC (92 % degradation in 120 min) under visible light illumination. Thus, the proposed strategy delivered an innovative insight for dual purpose of CDBHCF nanocomposite: as a fluorescent probe for real time monitoring and as a photocatalyst for removal of pollutants via simple photocatalytic degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, demands of an increasingly populous world have caused a significant expansion in industrial and agricultural operations. This in turn generated large quantities of wastewater with varied range of organic and inorganic contaminants, imposing severe threat to sustainable development and ecosystem security [1,2]. As all the life forms are immediately impacted by aquatic pollution, therefore detection and treatment of such water pollutants is imperative to alleviate the consequent environmental menace.

Methyl parathion (MP) is a kind of organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) which has been extensively used to protect agricultural crops from insects’ attack. However, abusive use of MP has adversely contaminated water and food supply chain owing to its bioaccumulation and low solubility [3,4]. It is reported that enrichment of MP in human body inhibits the activity of acetylcholinesterase leading to serious health issues related to nervous, digestive, respiratory and reproductive systems. Although many countries have banned use of toxic MP but the safe permissible limit of MP in drinking water has been restricted to 2 ppm by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [5,6]. On other side, continuous development of nuclear energy emanated release of radioactive uranium into natural aquatic system caused by uranium mining, military activities and nuclear industry emissions [7,8]. Typically, uranium exists in different forms (ranging from + 2 to + 6 oxidation states), among which uranyl ion (UO2 2+) is the most stable form of uranium. As it is water soluble, it can easily migrate to the aquatic environment and ultimately reach to human body causing irreversible internal damages such as nephropathy and osteosarcoma. The health-based value for UO2 2+ in drinking water has been restricted to 15 ppb as per World Health Organization (WHO) [9]. Therefore, it is vital to establish simple, efficient and safe monitoring method for detection of hazardous MP and UO2 2+ in order to ensure the safety of mankind and the global ecosystem.

A number of different analytical techniques have been established for the detection of environmental pollutants, amongst which fluorescence detection method come out ahead owing to their unique features including simple operation, low cost, high efficiency, quick response time and real-time monitoring [10–13]. Carbon dots (CDs), a newly emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, offers significant advantages with good optical properties, biocompatibility, physicochemical properties, photochemical stability and versatility [10,14,15]. Fluorescence emission is a characteristic feature of CDs which makes it an appealing probe in fluorescence sensing. Moreover, incorporation of CDs in different solid host materials has provided CDs with interesting fluorescence properties and more fluorescence stability in solution phase [16,17]. The CDs in-matrix strategy provided an exclusive platform for the development of CDs based sensors with enhanced optical properties which further simplify the real applications. So far, different nanomaterials have been employed as host material for CDs including metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), silica, zeolites, boehmite (BH) nanoparticles, polymers, xerogels, etc. [18–22]. BH nanoparticles are one of the polymorphs of aluminium oxide hydroxide with chemical formula γ-AlOOH which is widely used as photoluminescent compound, flame retardant, catalyst support, reinforcement filler in ceramic composites, chemical modifier, adsorbent, and so on [23,24]. These BH nanoparticles exhibited cubic orthorhombic construction comprising two sheets of octahedral chain with aluminium ions core. Surface of the BH nanoparticles encompasses high aggregation of OH groups which allows adhesion of CDs to the surface of BH nanoparticles through hydrogen bonding [25,26]. Moreover, the non-toxic nature, low cost, high surface area and porosity of BH nanoparticles makes it suitable host material for CDs which in turn prevent aggregation, improved sustainability and photostability of CDs.

Scarce reports are available on the use of BH nanoparticles as a host material for CDs. Recently, Roy et al. have tailored CDs over the surface of BH nanoparticles (CDBH) using in-situ approach for the fluorescence detection of lethal Cr(VI) in water and living cells [27]. Similarly, in another work, Roy et al. reported a handheld fluorometric probe made up of CD decorated BH (CD@BH) nanostructure incorporated in a layer of poly vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene (PVDF-HFP) membrane for rapid and facile monitoring of Cr(VI) [28]. The constructed device was found to emit excellent blue fluorescence upon UV excitation and can detect Cr(VI) in a selective and sensitive manner. However, Sobhani et al. developed a fluorescent probe based on green-synthesized CDs and mesoporous BH nanoparticles for the sensitive detection of toxic imipramine drug present in aqueous media [25]. Herein, Valerian roots were employed as source of carbon to produce CDs. While, Rezaei et al. harnessed Tragacanth Gum for the preparation of CDs and utilized to modify the mesoporous surface of BH nanoparticles [26]. The resultant assay was used for the fluorescence detection of trace amount of doxorubicin drug. Motivated by these facts, there remains ample opportunity to explore CDs incorporated BH nanoparticles as a sensory material for selective and sensitive detection of poisonous MP and UO2 2+ in water samples.

Furthermore, sensors based on magnetic nanomaterials have attracted considerable interests due to significant improvement in sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method. In addition, the magnetic sensor can be easily collected and rapidly separated using an external magnetic field. Thus, incorporation of magnetism into the sensor would be a good choice [19,20]. Cobalt ferrite viz. CoFe2O4 (CF) nanoparticles are well known magnetic cubic ferrite widely used in biomedical applications, photocatalysis, sensing, electronic devices, etc. owing to their large availability, stable crystalline structure, magnetic anisotropy, low cost, visible-light activity, and eco-friendly nature [29,30]. The integration of CF not only provide magnetism but also imparts improved photocatalytic activity to the nanomaterials. Therefore, CF could be integrated with a system that comprise of fluorescent CDs supported over a solid host material to obtain a magnetically responsive sensor as well as photocatalyst.

Inspired by the aforementioned facts, current study described the fabrication of novel CDBHCF nanocomposites, that consist of CDs, BH and CF nanoparticles, using facile hydrothermal method. Firstly, novel CDs were prepared via green synthetic approach based on one-pot hydrothermal carbonization using the green precursor punarnava or hogweed (Boerhavia diffusa) leaves as raw materials. Boerhavia diffusa is one of the most commonly found weed and a well-known herb used to rejuvenate whole body. Thus, use of Boerhavia diffusa leaves for the production of CDs is economically viable and eco-friendly. In the next step, CDs were immobilized onto the solid surface of BH nanoparticles to produce CDBH via hydrogen bonding. Later, the CDBH nanocomposites were combined with CF using hydrothermal method to produce a series of CDBHCFx with varied amount of CF nanoparticles, where x = 50, 100, 150 mg of CF. The as-synthesized CDBHCF nanocomposites were utilized for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of perilous MP pesticide and UO2 2+ in aqueous sample. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared CDBHCF nanocomposites was also examined using tetracycline (TC) antibiotic as model contaminant in aqueous medium. All the results demonstrated that the CDBHCF could be employed as multifunctional materials for the potential applications in the areas of environmental system.

All chemical reagents and solvents used were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Uranyl nitrate hexahydrate (UO2(NO3)2·6H2O) was obtained from Merck. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), Aluminium nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O) and Oxalic acid (C2H2O4) was purchased from CDH Pvt. Ltd. Tetracycline hydrochloride, hydrogen peroxide (30 % w/v) were supplied by TCI chemicals. Ethylenediamine was procured from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd. Methyl parathion (MP) was bought from local market in Chandigarh, India. Distilled water was utilized throughout all the experimental measurements.

The as-prepared samples were investigated by employing various characterization techniques. The crystallinity and phase purity of samples was analyzed by Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Panalytical’s X’Pert Pro Diffractometer outfitted with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) in 2θ range of 5°-80°. The identification of surface functional groups in fabricated samples was achieved by recording Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra using Perkin Elmer Spectrophotometer in scan range of 4000–400 cm−1. To explore the surface states and elemental composition of samples, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was employed using PHI 5000 VersaProbe III instrument. The magnetic measurements of samples were explored using Microsense EZ7 Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM). To visualize the morphology of synthesized materials, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) was utilized using Hitachi-SU8010 instrument. The fluorescence studies were performed using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) equipped with quartz cell of 1 cm path length. Photocatalytic degradation was studied using JASCO V-750 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer.

CDs were fabricated by employing hydrothermal treatment as follows: 10 g of dried Boerhavia diffusa leaves were crushed in 120 ml of distilled water and transferred to Teflon lined autoclave which was subsequently heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally, the obtained mixture was centrifuged followed by filtration of collected supernatant with a Whatman 42 filter paper. The resulting solution was then subjected to dialysis against distilled water for two days to obtain the purified CDs solution which was finally dried in oven at 70 °C to get the powdered form and stored at low temperature until further use. Additionally, CF was synthesized through conventional sol–gel method using citric acid as a gelling agent. In a typical experiment, metal nitrate salts were dissolved in minimum amount of distilled water and heated at 90 °C with continuous stirring. Afterwards, citric acid solution and ethylene glycol was added to resultant solution and magnetically stirred at 90 °C until gel is formed. The obtained gel precursor was then transferred to an alumina crucible and finally annealed at 400 °C for 2 h to procure CF nanoparticles [31].

The CDBHCFx nanocomposites were prepared according to procedure reported in literature with slight modification [27]. Firstly, CD and CF were dispersed in distilled water using ultrasonic treatment and labeled as solution A. In a separate beaker, aluminium nitrate was dissolved in distilled water and pH of this solution was adjusted to 12 using ethylenediamine and labeled as solution B. Both the solutions A and B were mixed and homogenized through magnetic stirring for 3 h. The obtained solution was then transferred to Teflon autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 170 °C for 20 h. The resulting product was centrifuged and washed with distilled water, followed by drying in vacuum oven at 70° C. The magnetic nanocomposites were fabricated with three different amounts of CF: 50, 100 and 150 mg, which were labeled as CDBHCF50, CDBHCF100 and CDBHCF150, respectively. Fig. 1 illustrates the synthetic route of CDBHCFx nanocomposites. For comparative study, composite of CD and BH was prepared by similar method without adding CF, denoted as CDBH nanocomposite.

In order to investigate the fluorescence properties of CDBHCF, stock solution of CDBHCF (0.25 mg/mL) was prepared in distilled water and mixed ultrasonically to form an evenly dispersed suspension. In a typical run, different amount of analyte solution (UO2 2+ and MP) was introduced into calibrated test tube and diluted to 4 mL using distilled water. Then 1 mL of CDBHCF solution was added to achieve total volume of 5 mL for fluorescence measurements. Following this, the solutions were mixed thoroughly and transferred to 1 cm × 1 cm fluorescence quartz cell for analysis. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 340 nm to 640 nm under excitation of 330 nm with both slit width of 5 nm at fixed scan rate of 600 nm/min. All experiments were repeated three times to certify statistical accuracy.

The photodegradation of TC (50 mg/L) was studied under 150 W Xenon lamp as irradiation source. In a typical process, pH of TC solution (100 mL) was adjusted to 2.5 owing to the formation of large amount of hydroxyl radicals at this pH [32]. Afterwards, 10 mg of as-synthesized catalyst was introduced into this solution. The above solution was magnetically stirred in dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Following this, optimized amount of H2O2 as oxidant was added and reaction mixture was irradiated with visible light source. Sample aliquots were periodically collected and catalyst was separated using an external magnet. The obtained supernatant was analyzed using UV–Vis Spectrophotometer to monitor the concentration of TC in reaction mixture.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the synthesized nanomaterials was investigated by performing XRD analysis. Fig. 2 showed XRD patterns of CDs, BH, CDBH, CF and CDBHCFx nanocomposites (x = 50, 100 and 150 mg of CF). A broad peak at 2θ value of 24.2° was observed in the diffraction pattern of CDs that corresponds to (002) lattice planes of carbon based materials indicating disordered graphitic core for the carbon particles with amorphous nature [33]. For pure BH, the strong diffraction peaks at 15.9°, 29.2°, 38.2°, 46.3°, 49.7°, 51.0°, 55.1°, 60.2°, 64.8, 66.3° and 72.1° are caused by diffraction from the (020), (120), (031), (131), (200), (220), (151), (080), (002), (022) and (251) planes of orthorhombic phase of γ-AlOOH (JCPDS card no. 21–1307) [34]. The nano BH adopts lamellar structure within orthorhombic structure where four tetrahedral oxygen and two hydroxyl groups are bounded around octahedral aluminium. Each layer constitutes the (OAlOH-HOAlO) stacking sequence attached via hydrogen bonding resulting in formation of zig-zag chains. The characteristic diffraction peaks for CF were observed at 2θ value of 31.1°, 35.2°, 43.8°, 53.5°, 56.4° and 63.6° corresponding to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) cubic planes of CF. The observed diffraction peaks were well consistent with the cubic spinel structured CoFe2O4 (JCPDS card no. 01–1121) [31]. Moreover, the different CDBHCFx nanocomposites displayed the common peaks of BH and CF suggested that BH and CF exist as two different phases in the composites. However, the CDs peaks were not obviously observed in the XRD patterns of nanocomposites due to their non-crystalline structure. The diffraction peaks of BH and CF in nanocomposite and pure forms were in good agreement with the standard database which confirmed the successful formation of nanocomposite. Further, absence of any impurity peak assured the complete transformation of precursors into desired compound after hydrothermal treatment.

FT-IR measurements were performed to investigate the availability of various functionalities present on the surface of CDs, BH, CDBH, CF and CDBHCFx nanocomposites (x = 50, 100 and 150 mg of CF). As portrayed in Fig. 3 , in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the O–H stretching was noted at 3510 cm−1 and the bands at 2928 and 2826 cm−1 were attributed to –CH stretching vibrations. The C=O stretching vibrations were identified at 1750 cm−1. Notably, the peaks ∼ 1650 and 1380 cm−1 were assigned to C=C stretching vibrations. The bands detected at 1122 cm−1 signified the C–O stretching in CDs [35]. On another side, the FT-IR spectrum of BH nanoparticles comprise of two strong bands located at 3300 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 associated with asymmetric and symmetric modes of O–H, respectively. The bands located at 1070 and 1160 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric Al–O–H vibrations, respectively. The torsional modes present at 742, 620 and 480 cm−1 were occurred due to vibrations of AlO6 [34]. However, the FT-IR spectra of CF evidenced two prominent bands at ∼ 580 cm−1 (tetrahedral metal–oxygen bond stretching) and 420 cm−1 (octahedral metal–oxygen bond stretching), apart from a broad peak at ∼ 3300 cm−1 representing O–H stretching vibration mode of surface adsorbed water molecules [31,34]. Although, CDs peaks were not present in the composites as intense characteristic peaks of BH and CF nanoparticles masks the featured bands of CDs. But, all the CDBHCFx nanocomposites (x = 50, 100 and 150 mg of CF) contained characteristic peaks of BH and CF, which confirmed that all the nanocomposites were successfully engineered. Thus, there is a good conformity between the FT-IR and XRD results which confirm the synthesis procedure.

XPS is an effective characterization method to evaluate the elemental composition and chemical states of the atomic species in CDBHCF100. The full survey spectrum in Fig. 4 (a) confirmed the presence of Al, C, O, Fe and Co elements in CDBHCF100. Fig. 4(b-f) depicted high resolution spectra of Al 2p, C 1 s, O 1 s, Fe 2p, and Co 2p, respectively. The Al 2p high resolution spectra can be fitted with one peak at 74.49 eV attributed to presence of Al3+ species [34]. The high resolution C 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three binding energies of 285.3, 286.1 and 289.5 eV, corresponding to sp2 (C–C), sp3 (C–C and C–H) and C=O bonds, respectively [33]. The O 1 s spectra in Fig. 4(d) can be decomposed into three peaks at 530.1 and 533.1 eV ascribed to metal–oxygen (M−O i.e. Al/Fe/Co–O bonds), and the hydroxyl (O–H) groups, respectively [34]. In Fig. 4(e), two clear peaks at 710.1 and 724.5 eV for Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 can be observed in the Fe 2p spectrum. The peak at 710.1 eV was attributed to Fe3+ cation sited at the octahedral site in the spinel structure, while the peak at 724.5 eV confirmed the presence of Fe2+ at the tetrahedral site in the spinel structure. Besides, in the Co 2p spectrum, the primary peaks were observed at the binding energies of 780.1 eV (Co 2p3/2) and 798.3 eV (Co 2p1/2) which demonstrated the presence of Co2+ ion in the spinel crystal system [31]. The XPS analysis showed the successful incorporation of all the three components, namely, CDs, BH and CF.

The morphology and shape of synthesized nanocomposites were explored using FESEM analysis. Fig. S1 displays the FESEM images of CDBH nanocomposite which demonstrated the porous surface of BH substrate with irregular elongated bean like shape of BH particles with an average size of ∼ 35 nm. CDs were captured in the pores of BH to form CDBH nanocomposite. EDX analysis along with mapping of CDBH revealed dispersive arrangement of encapsulated CDs into BH substrate and validated the presence of only C, Al and O elements in nanocomposite. Further, Fig. 5 demonstrated FESEM micrographs of CDBHCF100 which showed uniform decoration of spherical shaped CF nanoparticles (average size ∼ 18 nm) over the surface of CDBH nanocomposite. The agglomerations of nanoparticles were formed as result of inherent magnetic character of CF nanoparticles. Furthermore, EDX examination confirmed the existence of only desired elements (C, Al, O, Co and Fe) in CDBHCF nanocomposite, implying the purity of synthesized material (Fig. 5(b)). The elemental mapping via FESEM revealed the homogenous distribution of CF over the matrix of CDBH nanocomposite (Fig. 5(c-g)).

The optical properties of as-prepared CDBHCF were investigated by recording UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CDBHCF100. Before commencing the detailed scrutinization of fluorescence properties, the PL spectra of all the fabricated CDBHCFx nanocomposites (x = 50, 100 and 150 mg of CF) were recorded (Fig. S2). Owing to the synergism established between newly constructed CDs and CF interface, maximum intensity was recorded in case of CDBHCF100 nanocomposite. Therefore, CDBHCF100 was opted for all PL experiments. As portrayed in Fig. 6 (a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of nanocomposite exhibited strong absorption peak at 228 nm which corresponded to π-π* transition of conjugated C=C bond, confirming the aromatic structure in the core of nanocomposite. Another weak shoulder in absorption spectrum was credited to n-π* transition of oxygen containing functional groups which also influenced the surface state of material [10]. Moreover, the PL spectra of CDBHCF100 nanocomposite demonstrated that PL emission occurred at 430 nm with the ideal excitation wavelength at 330 nm, revealing green fluorescence of aqueous solution of CDBHCF100 nanocomposite (inset of Fig. 6(a)) [33].

To further appraise the optical properties, the PL spectra of CDBHCF100 aqueous solution was assessed at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 nm to 460 nm with a 10 nm increment, as displayed in Fig. 6(b). The main PL emission peak displayed red shift with increase in excitation wavelength. The PL intensity was initially increasing and then followed by decrease in intensity, with maximum emission observed at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. These emission results revealed the excitation-dependent behavior of CDBHCF100 which might be due to particle size distribution and various functional groups present in CDBHCF100 [8,33].

Additionally, UV–vis DRS spectroscopy was employed to assess the band gap of CD, BH, CDBH and CDBHCFx nanocomposites (x = 50, 100 and 150 mg of CF) responsible for their photocatalytic activities. Fig. 6(c) portrayed DRS spectra of all fabricated materials in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The results showed that BH has less absorption ability in visible range and therefore could not contribute extensively to visible light-driven photocatalytic degradation. Interestingly, with the encapsulation of CDs into BH, visible light absorption significantly improved with enhancement in overall absorption intensity which attributed to strong light harvesting capacity of CDs. After the decoration of CF on CDBH nanocomposite, CDBHCF nanocomposites presented a red shift in absorption band edge as compared to CDBH, with largest shift identified for CDBHCF150 nanocomposite owing to maximum content of highly responsive visible light photocatalyst. The band gap of synthesized materials was determined from DRS spectra using Tauc’s plot, as described in equation (1), [32]:
(1)
α h ν = A
( h ν -
E g
)
1 / 2

Where α, h, ν,A and Eg represents absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, the frequency of absorbed photon, the proportionality constant, and optical band gap, respectively. Plot of (αhν)2 vs. hν gives the band gap values by extending the straight line to the hν axis, as displayed in Fig. S3. The computed optical band gap values of BH, CDBH, CF, CDBHCF50, CDBHCF100 and CDBHCF150 were 2.74 eV, 1.95 eV, 1.4 eV, 1.7 eV, 1.61 eV and 1.51 eV, respectively. With the encapsulation of CDs into BH, the band gap of BH decreased to 1.95 eV and shifted towards visible region. With the loading of CF over CDBH, band gap further narrowed owing to brilliant visible light activity of CF. It was observed that increase in CF amount led to increase in visible light absorption range by varying degrees, thus decreasing the band gap value of corresponding CDBHCFx nanocomposite. However, the obtained Eg values suggested that all CDBHCFx nanocomposites were responsive towards visible light irradiation.

From both economic and environmental point of view, recycling of material following its employment for environmental remediation is of utmost importance. Room temperature hysteresis loops of all the synthesized magnetic materials (CF, CDBHCF50, CDBHCF100 and CDBHCF150) were recorded using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) in applied magnetic field range of −10,000 to + 10,000 Oe, as depicted in Fig. 6(d). VSM analysis revealed the ferromagnetic behavior of all samples and the corresponding magnetic parameters including saturation magnetization (Ms), coercivity (Hc) and remanence (Mr) and squareness ratio (S=Mr/Ms) were evaluated and given in Table 1 . Noticeable change was observed in Ms value of CF and CDBHCF nanocomposites. This reduction in magnetic behavior of nanocomposite was due to alliance of CF with non-magnetic CDBH nanocomposite. However, increased CF content in CDBHCF nanocomposite tends to augment Ms value of corresponding ternary nanocomposite. All the CDBHCFx nanocomposites possessed non zero squareness ratio owing to coherent magnetic character of CF which further validated the ferromagnetic character of samples and ensured their facile separation using external magnet [29,31]. Moreover, these results stated that synthesized materials assisted in control of secondary pollutants in water owing to their recoverability in interest of clean environment. The abovementioned outcomes made clear that CDBHCF100 possessed appreciable photoluminescence along with sufficient magnetic behavior for easy recovery. Therefore, CDBHCF100 was considered as best possible choice for PL measurements.

Stability of a material usually depends upon the different external factors; therefore, it is worthwhile to explore the effect of pH, salt concentration and storage time on the PL properties of CDBHCFx nanocomposites. As displayed in Fig. S4(a), the influence of pH on PL intensity of CDBHCF100 was inspected by varying pH from 2 to 11. The PL intensity decreased with increase in pH value which may be due to substantial protonation at low pH value, leading to sufficient hydrophilicity, dispersibility and thus stability in aqueous medium. Alongwith, due to solvation of ferrite nanoparticles in acidic medium, proper breaching of light through solution resulted in augmented PL intensity [7,33]. On the contrary, in alkaline medium, electrostatic repulsion aroused due to negatively charged surface of nanomaterial that led to decrease in PL intensity. These results indicated that the CDBHCF100 nanocomposite will perform efficiently for real wastewater which is generally acidic in nature. Moreover, the influence of electrolyte concentration was investigated by recording PL intensity in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl with varying range of 10–5000 mM (Fig. S4(b)). No significant change in PL intensity was witnessed with increase in electrolyte concentration owing to no interference between sensor and electrolyte. The proposed sensor thus exhibited utmost stability even towards extreme ionic strength conditions. To further evaluate the performance of CDBHCF for detection assays, effect of storage time was examined by monitoring the PL intensity of CDBHCF100 at an interval of 5 days (Fig. S4(c)). The results displayed stable PL intensity of CDBHCF100 system even after 30 days, indicating its great potential for detection purposes in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection selectivity and sensitivity of CDBHCFx for MP pesticide
ce:section.ce:para: With increase in agricultural industrialization, the irrational use of pesticides in modern cultivation and crop production poses a serious risk to both humans and environment. Therefore, detection of pesticides is of great significance to administer polluted wastewater for environmental safety. In this concern, CDBHCF100 was employed for detection of MP in aqueous medium. Selectivity of the proposed sensor was evaluated by parallel measurements of PL response against 10 different pesticides namely, chlorpyrifos (CP), dichlorvos (DCV), dicofol (DF), dimethoate (DM), fipronil (FP), imidacloprid (IMD), malathion (MT), monocrotophos (MC) and MP. As depicted in Fig. 7 (a), PL response of CDBHCF100 system was highly quenched in presence of MP whereas influence of other pesticides on PL response was negligible. This showed specific recognition ability of CDBHCF100 towards MP, enabling it to distinguish MP from other pesticides. The high selectivity of proposed sensor towards MP was appraised by anti-interference experiments in presence of other possible interferents in solution (Fig. 7(b)). The fluorescence quenching effect still existed even in media with co-existing pesticides and common interfering salts, revealing excellent anti-interference ability of CDBHCF100 for MP pesticide detection in real matrices.

To further explore the relationship between pesticide concentration and PL intensity of sensor, PL response of sensor was recorded upon injection of different concentrations of pesticide ranging from 0 to 5 μM (Fig. 7(c)) and the quantitative relation was examined by Stern-Volmer (SV) equation (2), [22]:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q

Where, F0 and F represent PL intensities of CDBHCF100 in absence and presence of analyte, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Q] is concentration of analyte. The PL quenching increased with increase in concentration of MP exhibiting good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9998) with KSV value estimated to be 4.892 × 10+6 M−1 as shown in Fig. 7(d). The LOD was evaluated using the formula 3σ/ KSV, where σ represents the standard deviation and LOD was found to be 22.4 nM [36]. The results confirmed the proficient ability of CDBHCF100 as a fluorescent sensor for MP with good selectivity and sensitivity. The inset of Fig. 7(d) displayed the visual change in fluorescence of CDBHCF100 in the presence of MP. Further, the performance of CDBHCF sensor was compared with other fluorescent probes for MP detection. The current sensor offered superior sensing platform with improved LOD than rest of the methods for the detection of MP in water samples, as displayed in Table 2
[37–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection selectivity and sensitivity of CDBHCF for UO2 2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of uranyl ions is of high importance owing to its serious chemical toxicity and biotoxicity. Therefore, to discover the potential of CDBHCF100 as a fluorescent probe for UO2 2+, PL responses of CDBHCF100 was investigated towards different metal ions existing in natural water, including Al3+, K+, As3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr4+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Na+ and UO2 2+ ions. Among these ions, fluorescence of CDBHCF100 was quenched only by UO2 2+ while no appreciable difference in PL intensity was observed for other metal ions, suggesting excellent selectivity of CDBHCF100 towards UO2 2+ ion (Fig. 8 (a)). Moreover, the anti-interference studies revealed that even presence of different interferents having ten folds concentration does not affect the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDBHCF100 towards UO2 2+ ion (Fig. 8(b)). The results emphasized CDBHCF100 as good candidate to monitor UO2 2+ with great selectivity and sensitivity in aqueous medium.

The effect of varying UO2 2+ concentrations on the PL intensity of CDBHCF100 was investigated and their correlation was determined by Stern-Volmer (SV) equation. The CDBHCF100 nanocomposite has a good linear relationship with satisfactory correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) and KSV of 7.299 × 10+6 M−1 in concentration range of 0–4 μM UO2 2+, as depicted in Fig. 8(c, d). The LOD was found to be 4.4 nM that insinuated the application of CDBHCFx system as highly sensitive fluorescence sensor for detection of UO2 2+. The inset of Fig. 8(d) displayed the visual change in fluorescence of CDBHCF100 in the presence of UO2 2+.

Comparison of proposed fluorescence sensor with previously reported UO2 2+ ion sensors is given in Table 2 [8,41–43]. The better LOD achieved for designed sensor presented an effective opportunity to quantitatively monitor the UO2 2+ ions in real environmental solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the possible detection mechanism of PL quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection mechanism using CDBHCF100 as probe towards MP and UO2 2+ was investigated in detail. The trend of fluorescence lifetime is the most reliable way to explore the quenching mechanism. Fluorescence quenching of a fluorophore occurred through different processes, broadly it can be divided into two parts: one is the interaction between quencher and the excited molecules of fluorescent substances; the other is the interaction between quencher and the ground state molecules of fluorescent substance, the former is called dynamic quenching, and the latter is static quenching. The primary difference between the two process lies in the fact that the fluorescence lifetime undergoes alterations in dynamic quenching, whereas it remains constant in the case of static quenching [44]. The fluorescence decays of CDBHCF100 at the emission maximum were initially recorded, both in the absence and presence of the analyte (MP or UO2 2+) (Fig. S5(a)). The fluorescence lifetime of pure CDBHCF100 was measured as 3.43 ns in the absence of any analyte, and this decay time remained unaffected even in the presence of MP (3.45 ns) or UO2 2+ (3.76 ns). The observed change in fluorescence decay time was not significant, thereby eliminating the possibility of dynamic quenching processes such as photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or Fӧrster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between analyte and CDBHCF100. Correspondingly, static quenching was further verified by UV–vis absorption studies as shown in Fig. S5(b, c). The absorption spectrum of CDBHCF100 probe, analyte (MP or UO2 2+) and probe + analyte was compared and absorption spectra showed enhancement upon interaction of CDBHCF100 sensor with analyte (MP or UO2 2+). Further, in the presence of either analyte (MP or UO2 2+) change in absorption maxima was observed with slight red shift, thus authenticate the static mechanism for quenching by both MP and UO2 2+ [45].

For quenching via inner filter effect (IFE) in static quenching mechanism, the UV absorption peaks of analyte (MP or UO2 2+) should overlap with excitation or emission peak of sensor [46,47]. As seen from Fig. 9 (a), the absorption spectrum of MP depicts absorption band at 292 nm which apparently intersects the excitation band of CDBHCF100 sensor. Due to this spectral overlap, some part of excitation light for sensor was absorbed and thus shielded by MP, suggesting the IFE mechanism for quenching of MP. On the other hand, the absorption spectrum of UO2 2+ showed overlapping with significant portion of the emission band of CDBHCF100 (Fig. 9(b)) and due to this overlap, fluorescence of sensor was absorbed by UO2 2+ ions. These results illustrated that the quenching of CDBHCF100 was due to IFE mechanism. Upon illumination by the external excitation source, the target analyte (MP and UO2 2+) competes with CDBHCF100 for light and disrupts the existing energy transfer process within CDBHCF100 sensor, consequently weakening its characteristic fluorescence emission. This competitive absorption relationship between the proposed sensor and both analytes leads to IFE in static quenching (Fig. 9(c)). Therefore, static quenching and IFE mechanisms were synergistically involved in the fluorescence quenching of CDBHCF100 sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CDBHCF in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most essential factors in designing fluorescence sensors is to validate its feasibility for practical application in real matrices. The CDBHCF100 nanocomposite was thus used for detection of analyte (MP and UO2 2+) in real water samples by using standard spiking treatment. The water samples were collected from Sukhna Lake (Chandigarh, India) and Ganga River (Rishikesh, India). Afterwards, samples were filtered and spiked with different concentrations of analytes for real sample analysis. As shown in Table 3 , the % recovery ranged from 94 % to 102 % for spiked samples, with relative standard deviation values less than 4 %. The results implied satisfactory recovery percentage with excellent accuracy and precision of the proposed method in different samples, showing that the sensor served as a reliable approach for fluorescence sensing in realistic context.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of photocatalytic performance towards TC removal
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic efficiencies of fabricated samples were explored for degradation of TC under visible light irradiation. The progress of reaction was appraised by recording UV–vis absorption spectra. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out in presence of H2O2 as oxidizing agent, following photo-Fenton degradation mechanism. The overall degradation achieved was calculated using equation (3), [32]:
(3)
%
D e g r a d a t i o n =
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100

Where, C0 and Ct represent the concentration of TC at the irradiation time of 0 min and t min, respectively. Further, the degradation kinetics was determined by data fitting using pseudo first order reaction kinetics equation (4), [31]:
(4)
- l n
C t
C 0
= k t

Where, k is first order rate constant. To achieve best photocatalytic results, effects of reaction parameters such as catalyst dosage and initial pH were inspected and optimized reaction conditions were ascertained for all photocatalytic experiments. Fig. S6(a) describes the influence of pH on photodegradation activity of nanocomposites. It has been worth noting that acidic pH conditions have already been proved to be most optimal for photo-Fenton degradation transpired from H2O2-ferrite based photocatalysts. Therefore, degradation was carried out in acidic pH media ranging from 2 to 4 to decide on specific pH value so as to accomplish maximum degradation. The highest degradation of TC was obtained at pH=2.5 and it was attributed to augmented generation of radicals at this pH. This was believed to be due to boost in photo-Fenton reaction upon addition of H2O2 as well as easy decomposition of H2O2 at low pH values [32]. Likewise, to comprehend the impact of catalyst dosage, degradation experiments of TC were performed using different amount of catalyst varying from 0.05 g/L to 0.2 g/L at fixed pH value of 2.5 (Fig. S6(b)). Initially, as catalyst dosage is increased from 0.05 g/L to 0.1 g/L, TC removal efficiency improved and then essentially remain unchanged and reduced upon increasing catalyst concentration to 0.15 g/L and 0.2 g/L, respectively. The results suggested maximum active site exposure of photocatalyst to visible light at catalyst dosage of 0.1 g/L which further led to higher degree of TC removal [48]. Therefore, pH of 2.5 and catalyst dosage of 0.1 g/L were opted as optimized reaction parameters for photocatalytic degradation studies of TC.

The comparative photocatalytic degradation performance of pure boehmite and synthesized composites via photo-Fenton mechanism is portrayed in Fig. 10 (a). The results demonstrated that BH exhibited very low degradation activity of 14 % towards TC in 120 min that substantiated negligible involvement of BH in degradation mechanism. Upon modification with CDs, slightly improved degradation of 38 % was achieved with CDBH composite due to brilliant light harvesting characteristics of CDs. Various functional groups present on CDs were responsive to light illumination which contributed to the photogeneration of additional e−/h+ pairs that participated in degradation mechanism [49]. Moreover, introduction of CF to CDBH resulted in sharp increment in overall degradation of TC upto 78 %, 87 % and 92 % for CDBHCF50, 100 and 150, respectively. Owing to excellent photocatalytic properties of CF on account of its suitable band gap between conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), increase in CF content eventually led to augmented photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CDBHCF nanocomposites.

The corresponding ‘-ln(Ct/C0) vs. t’ plots are presented in Fig. 10(b) and the calculated rate constant values are mentioned in Table 4 . As depicted from plots, CDBH exhibited about 6 folds boosted degradation rate in comparison to BH as a result of additional photogenerated charge carriers produced with the aid of light source. Besides, the rate constant values obtained for CDBHCF50, CDBHCF100 and CDBHCF150 clearly demonstrated the accelerated photodegradation performance with addition of CF. The maximum rate of degradation was presented by CDBHCF150 having rate constant value of 27.8 × 10−3 min−1 which was about 15 and 93 times the rate constant determined for CDBH and BH, respectively. The improved photo-Fenton degradation employing CDBHCFx as a photocatalyst was established due to synergistic effect of all three components in CDBHCFx nanocomposite. CDs and CF concomitantly enhanced the visible light utilization of BH nanoparticles ensuing from the smaller band gap established in CDBHCF. These results certified that the alliance of individual components in synthesized nanocomposite displayed the synergistic ability to function as outstanding photocatalyst for photodegradation process in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probable photocatalytic degradation mechanism for TC
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the generation of reactive species in photocatalytic reaction, sequential radical scavenging experiments were performed with various scavengers: ammonium oxalate (AO) for holes (h+), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) for superoxide (O2 ·−) radicals and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for hydroxyl (HO·) radicals. As shown in Fig. S7, the performance of CDBHCF100 catalyst for TC degradation was changed in all three systems. Overall degradation efficiency was reduced to 47 %, 72 % and 50 % in presence of AO, p-BQ and IPA, respectively. These results suggested the major involvement of h+ and HO· radicals in degradation mechanism, with slight participation of O2 ·− radicals. Similar results were also been reported in literature that showed the involvement of these active species for photocatalytic degradation mechanism [50].

In a conventional degradation mechanism, electrons from the VB of CF migrated to its CB under the influence of visible light and resulted in generation of electron-hole (e−/h+) pairs (equation (5)). The holes (h+) in VB transferred to the surface of catalyst and oxidized H2O molecules or hydroxyl (HO−) ions to produce hydroxyl (HO·) radicals (equations (6) and (7)). In unison, the photoinduced electrons (e−) reacted with O2 to form superoxide (O2 ·−) radicals (equation (8)), [51].

Nevertheless, for thorough understanding of mechanism, the band positions of CF were determined using Mulliken electronegativity theory following equations (9) and (10), [31]:
(9)

Where, Ee, χ and Eg represents energy of free electrons (4.5 eV) vs. standard hydrogen electrode, semiconductor’s Mulliken electronegativity and band gap, respectively. EVB and ECB are the potentials of semiconductor in VB and CB, respectively. From literature, the value of χ for CF is 5.81 eV and therefore, EVB and ECB for CF were estimated to be 2.01 eV and 0.61 eV, respectively. The outcomes suggested that since redox potential of O2 ·−/O2 (−0.33 eV) was higher than CB potential (0.61 eV) of photocatalyst, electrons could not participate in degradation mechanism [31]. On the other hand, VB edge potential was lower than oxidation potential of HO·/HO− (+1.99 eV), insinuating contribution of only hydroxyl radicals. In unison, upon addition of H2O2, generation of photo-Fenton’s system takes place on account of partial overlapping of Fe2+ 3d orbital with sp3 hybrid orbital of H2O2 that also contributes in expedition of photodegradation (equations (11), (12) and (13)) [31,33]. In detail, The Fe2+ reacted with H2O2 to generate Fe3+ which further got reduced to Fe2+ after utilizing photoinduced electrons, thus assisted in improving the charge carrier separation (equation (14)) [52]. Moreover, in the designed nanocomposite, firstly CDs served as good electron acceptors and then good electron reservoirs due to close contact between CF and CDs. The electrons in CB of CF were captured by CDs, thereby restraining recombination of photoinduced e−/h+ pairs. This effective charge separation created a unidirectional flow of electrons which resulted in enhanced degradation performance. These electrons have the excellent potential to decompose H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (HO·/H2O2; 0.87 eV vs. NHE) as presented in equation (15). CDs serving as electron pool facilitated the overall transport of charge carriers and thus improved the availability of charge carriers for photocatalytic process. Due to their carbon-based composition, CDs have the potential to adsorb TC molecule and exposed more reactive sites for photodegradation [53,54]. Concurrently, H2O2 reacted with generated hydroxyl radicals to produce O2 ·− radicals (equation (16)). These radicals acted as major reactive oxygen species and were responsible for degradation of TC into CO2, H2O and smaller products (equation (17)). The proposed mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of TC over CDBHCF nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 11 .

In this way, the construction of CDBHCF nanocomposite allowed prominent visible light harvesting and efficient separation of charge carriers that accelerated the photodegradation process of TC.

The reuse of materials is very important for reducing the testing cost and protecting environment, so the recyclability of CDBHCF was studied for both detection and removal applications.After performing PL measurements, the CDBHCF100 sensor was separated with aid of external magnet and washed repeatedly with ethanol before drying in oven. The PL of recovered sensor was then recorded and Fig. S8(a) showed good stability in PL response of CDBHCF100 upto four cycles. Additionally, the recycled CDBHCF100 exhibited the promising detection of UO2 2+ ions which validated the splendid reusable ability of CDBHCF in practical applications. Likewise, the recycling performance of CDBHCF100 in the removal of TC was evaluated for four consecutive photodegradation experiments. No considerable reduction in overall % degradation was observed on employing recovered catalysts, as displayed in Fig. S8(b). These results clearly substantiated the outstanding recycling performance of CDBHCF100 nanocomposite, making it promising material for practical application prospects.

In this investigation, biomass derived CDs based fluorescent probe has been constructed and successfully utilized for the detection of baneful MP pesticide and UO2 2+ in aqueous medium. The designed sensor can be exploited for the quantitative detection of MP and UO2 2+, having detection limit as low as 22.4 nM and 4.4 nM, respectively. The sensor exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability. The practical utility of sensor was certified by applying it in real water samples collected from different water bodies and satisfactory recoveries (>90 %) were achieved. Besides, the concocted nanocomposite also bestowed great potential in photodegradation, demonstrating 92 % removal of toxic TC antibiotic in 120 min using CDBHCF150 nanocomposite. The inherent magnetic character of synthesized nanocomposite assisted in high recovery of samples, suggesting its superior recoverability. Taking advantages from individual components, this work based on biomass derived CDs and magnetic CF offered a promising strategy for the detection and alleviation of water pollutants.
